coredata.dc:title: Tunable full-color solid-state fluorescent carbon dots for light emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Junli
Zheng, Jingxia
Yang, Yongzhen
Liu, Xuguang
Qiu, Jieshan
Tian, Yue
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) as a class of new fluorescent materials have attracted numerous attention. However, the solid-state photoluminescence quenching of CDs is still a big challenge. In this work, self-quenching-resistant solid-state fluorescent CDs are achieved, which exhibit tunable full-color solid-state emission without any other additional solid matrices. These CDs were prepared via one-step microwave method using phloroglucinol and urea as sources by regulating reactant ratio and microwave power. The maximum emission of CDs gradually shifts from 445 to 643 nm, and solid-state quantum yield of five typical CDs with blue (B), green (G), yellow (Y), orange (O) and red (R) emission reaches up 48.2%, 26.0%, 18.5%, 13.7% and 5.7%, respectively. Moreover, multicolor solid-solid fluorescence mechanism of CDs is investigated. The G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs show aggregation-induced red-shift behavior, while B-CDs exhibit unique crystallization-induced emission enhancement property. Finally, CDs are utilized as phosphors combing with ultraviolet chip to fabricate multicolor and white light emitting diodes (LEDs). The obtained white LEDs exhibit an adjustable correlated color temperature (3937–8588 K) and high color rendering index (80–88). This study provides a novel guidance for developing self-quenching resistant CDs with multicolor emission.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Solid-state fluorescence
Tunable full-color emission
Aggregation-induced red shift
Crystal-induced emission enhancement
Light emitting diodes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structure and surface property of B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs
Optical property and PL mechanism of B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs
Application of B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs in multicolor/white LEDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( U1710117 and 51972221 ) and Research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2020–051 and HGKY2019027 ).

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1710117 and 51972221) and Research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2020?051 and HGKY2019027).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Maximum emission peaks of CDs prepared at different microwave powers and PG/urea molar ratios. (b) Synthesis routes of typical five CDs by microwave treatment of PG and urea. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a–e) TEM (left) and HRTEM (right) images, insets in TEM images are particle size distribution of CDs. Raman spectra (f), FT-IR spectra (g) and XPS survey spectra (h) of five CDs. Inset in graph h is high resolution N1s XPS spectra of B-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) Photographs, (b) PL emission spectra, (c) excitation spectra and (d) Time-resolved PL spectra of five CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Fluorescent images of R- (a1), O- (a2), Y- (a3), G-CD (a4) DMSO solution with different concentrations under UV light. (b–e) Normalized PL spectra of solutions with different concentrations and powder for R-, O-, Y-, G-CDs under excitation of 365 nm. (f) Overlap between PL spectra of dilute solution (0.05 mg/mL) and absorption of concentrated solution (15 mg/mL). (g) ζ-Potential of G-, Y-, O-, R-CD aqueous solution at concentration of 0.05 mg/mL. (h) Fluorescence decay curves of five CD dilute solution and powder. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) Photos under daylight (up) and UV light (down), and (b) PL emission of B-CD DMSO solution and powders and (c) Fluorescence-decay curves. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic mechanism of multicolor solid-state emission for five CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Photographs (a), CIE coordinates (b) and emission spectra (c) of operating B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-LED. Photographs (d1, d2, d3), CIE coordinates (e1, e2, e3) and emission spectra (f1, f2, f3) of cold warm, pure and warm white LEDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Tunable full-color solid-state fluorescent carbon dots for light emitting diodes

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Taiyuan University, Taiyuan, 030032, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) as a class of new fluorescent materials have attracted numerous attention. However, the solid-state photoluminescence quenching of CDs is still a big challenge. In this work, self-quenching-resistant solid-state fluorescent CDs are achieved, which exhibit tunable full-color solid-state emission without any other additional solid matrices. These CDs were prepared via one-step microwave method using phloroglucinol and urea as sources by regulating reactant ratio and microwave power. The maximum emission of CDs gradually shifts from 445 to 643 nm, and solid-state quantum yield of five typical CDs with blue (B), green (G), yellow (Y), orange (O) and red (R) emission reaches up 48.2%, 26.0%, 18.5%, 13.7% and 5.7%, respectively. Moreover, multicolor solid-solid fluorescence mechanism of CDs is investigated. The G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs show aggregation-induced red-shift behavior, while B-CDs exhibit unique crystallization-induced emission enhancement property. Finally, CDs are utilized as phosphors combing with ultraviolet chip to fabricate multicolor and white light emitting diodes (LEDs). The obtained white LEDs exhibit an adjustable correlated color temperature (3937–8588 K) and high color rendering index (80–88). This study provides a novel guidance for developing self-quenching resistant CDs with multicolor emission.

Full-color solid-state carbon dots are synthesized for the first time without any other additional solid matrices by regulating molar ratio of precursors and microwave power.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: As a new star of carbon materials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much attention for their application in various fields, such as light emitting diodes (LED), fingerprint recognition, nanosensors and biomedicine, because of their excellent optical property, environmental friendliness, low cost and simple synthesis routes [1–3]. At present, most reports about CDs focus on the property of their solution. However, CDs in solid-state film or powder always suffer from serious aggregation induced quenching (AIQ) ascribed to excessive fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or direct π-π interactions, which limits application of CDs in solid-state fields [4].

In recent years, a lot of researches have established some approaches for resisting AIQ of CDs. The common method to avoid the undesirable AIQ is dispersing CDs into solid-state matrices, such as starch, salt, BaSO4, silica and polymer [5–10]. Qu group has made great contribution to development of solid-state luminescence of CDs. Typically, they fabricated CDs@BaSO4 hybrid phosphors, which exhibited excellent thermal- and photo-stability and remarkable resistance to strong acid/alkali [8]. Nevertheless, AIQ issue still happens at high loading concentration of CDs in matrices. In fact, embedding CDs in matrices does not actually eliminate the inherent AIQ characteristic of CDs [11]. In practical applications, high-performance luminescent devices require strong photoluminescence (PL) emission output, which requires high loading fraction of CDs in matrices. In addition, these common methods are accompanied by time-consuming multi-step procedures [12]. To solve this problem, some researchers show their interest in direct construction of CDs with solid-state emission without further treatments. For example, Chen et al. [13] fabricated solid-state yellow-emitting CDs with quantum yield (QY) of 35.0% using ethyl diamine and polyvinyl alcohol as sources, which retained on the surface of CDs and can effectively suppress AIQ effect. Zheng et al. [14] in our groups synthesized blue emissive CDs with high solid-state QY of 65.8% using citric acid and long-chain silane coupling agent. Zhang et al. [15] synthesized yellow emissive CDs with solid-state QY of 65% using a mixture of citric acid and l -cysteine in the presence of KCl, which induces uniform particle size of CDs. However, in most cases, CDs exhibit single emission. The luminous display and lighting fields require multicolor fluorescent materials as color converters. Therefore, the preparation of tuanble multicolor solid-state CDs are urgent and desirable.

In the pursuit of multicolor CDs-based emitters with solid-state fluorescence, many studies have been done in recent years. Shao et al. [16] developed a CDs/starch system for realizing solid-state red, yellow, green and blue fluorescence on the basis of gradient disperse of CDs in starch. Chang et al. [17] developed a time-controlled solvothermal route to prepare solid-state multicolor (from blue to orange) nanocarbon emitters comprising CDs and nano sheet. Zhan et al. [18] demonstrated the adjustment of emitting color, from blue to orange, of solid-state silica-coated CDs composites by controlling the loading fraction of CDs in silica. However, few reports, if any, have realized full-color solid-state luminescence of CDs without any external matrix. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a new system to synthesize solid-state CDs with tunable full-color emission in one single step.

In this work, solid-state fluorescent CDs with full-color emission were achieved by one-step pyrolysis of phloroglucinol (PG) and urea. To our best knowledge, it is the first demonstration for preparation of full-color emissive solid-state CDs without any other additional solid matrices. Here, urea plays two important roles: improving QY of CDs as nitrogen source, and isolating nascent CDs to avoid their direct contact because generated gas from decomposition of urea makes products form an inflated foam structure. By regulating reactant ratio and microwave power, the maximum emission peak of obtained CDs shifts from 445 to 643 nm, covering entire visible spectrum. Typical blue (B), green (G), yellow (Y), orange (O) and red (R) emissive CDs (B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs) exhibit considerable solid-state QY of 48.2%, 26.0%, 18.5%, 13.7% and 5.7%, respectively. Finally, taking advantages of outstanding optical properties of CDs, multicolor and white LEDs were realized using CDs as phosphors and violet (UV) chip as LEDs base. By mixing controlled amounts of B-, G-, R-CDs into curing agent, white LEDs with adjustable correlated color temperature (CCT) and high color rendering index (CRI) were fabricated. This study provides a general synthesis route to produce color-tunable solid-state emissive CDs, which is beyond the previous reports that solid-state emissive color of CDs was changed by different loading fractions of CDs in matrices.

PG dehydrate was purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. Urea was obtained from Tianjin Dongli District Tianda Chemical Reagent Factory. DMSO was offered from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd. Petroleum ether and ethanol were provided from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. Deionized water was made in the laboratory. All of the chemicals were used without further purification.

CDs were synthesized using PG dehydrate and urea as raw materials by one-step microwave method. In general, fixed amount of PG dehydrate (10 mmol, 1.6214 g), changing amount of urea (160, 80, 40, 20, 10, 5, 2.5 mmol) and deionized water (10 mL) were added into a glass breaker (100 mL), which was then sonicated for 3 min and stirred to form a uniform dispersion. The mixtures were heated at 144, 288, 464, 648 and 800 W for 15 min in a domestic microwave oven (G80F23N1P-M8(S0)). After reacted at 144 W when molar ratio of PG/urea was 1:1, obtained solution was quickly put in refrigerator at −80 °C for 30 min, then, solid-state products were put on filter paper and in an oven at 30 °C to remove excess water to obtain B-CDs. When microwave power was 288 W and reactant ratio was 1:16–1:2, solutions changed to powder during reaction. After natural cooling, these powders were ground and dispersed in deionized water, followed by centrifugation (10000 rpm, 10 min) with water washing for three times to remove by-products. The precipitation was collected, dried and ground to obtain G-, Y-CDs. When microwave power was 288 W and reactant ratio of UG/urea was 1:0.5, the resulting powder was collected and then dissolved in ethanol to remove large particles by 0.22 μm filter membrane. Solution was concentrated in a rotary evaporator and petroleum ether was injected to form mixture dispersion (volume ratio of petroleum ether/ethanol = 30:1), which was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. After being washed by eluent for three times, the precipitate was collected, dried and ground to obtain O-CDs. When power was 464 W and reactant ratio was 1:0.25, R-CDs were obtained, and subsequent processes were same for O-CDs only with changed eluent (volume ratio of petroleum ether/ethanol = 15:1).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were recorded on JEOL JEM-2010. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was obtained in DX-2700B, Dandong Haoyuan Instrument Co. LTD, operating with a Cu-Kα target, λ = 0.15406 nm, and a scanning rate of 4°/min for 2θ from 10° to 80°. Raman spectra were obtained in Horiba HR800 by excitation of 325 nm. Elemental analysis (EA) measurement was conducted on ELEMENTAR vario EL cube. Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR) spectra were collected on TENSOR27, BRUKER. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were obtained on Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250 xi. Zeta potential was measured using Malvern nano ZS90. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on Hitachi U3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer. PL spectra were collected on Horiba Fluoromax-4 luminescence spectrometer using a Xe lamp as excitation source. QY was measured on C9920-02G. The thermogravimetric (TG) and the derivative thermogravimetric (DTG) analyses were run in a thermal analyzer (NETZSCH, STA449 F3). The samples were heated from 30 to 900 °C at a constant heating rate of 10 °C/min under nitrogen. The fluorescence decay status and lifetime were recorded on Edinburgh FLS 1000 spectrometer. Emission spectra and parameters of LED were collected on PR655 Spectra scan. The colors of light were identified by CIE system.

Multicolor and white LEDs were fabricated by using B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs as single phosphor and BGR-CDs as composite phosphors, respectively, and with UV chip as LED base. First, phosphors were dispersed into optical sealant OE6250 A, after that, equal amount of optical sealant OE6250 B was added to form 10 mg/mL CDs/sealant dispersion. Then, CDs dispersing agent were dropped on inner wall of optical lens. After drying and curing under vacuum, optical lens including CDs were capped on UV chip to fabricate LEDs.

Multiple color emissive CDs were prepared using PG and urea as raw materials by adjusting microwave power and molar ratio of PG to urea. As shown in Fig. 1 a, at low microwave power (144 W), the obtained CDs display blue fluorescence no matter how molar ratio changes. When microwave power is increased to 288 W and above, the reactant ratio of PG to urea has a more remarkable influence than microwave power on luminescence color of CDs, and the increase in both of them leads to gradual red shift of maximum peaks. The maximum emission of CDs varies from 445 to 643 nm with the increase of microwave power and decrease of urea content. Five typical CDs, named as B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs, were chosen as models for further investigation, whose synthesis conditions are as shown in Fig. 1b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and surface property of B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM images of CDs in Fig. 2 a–e reveal that the CDs are spherical with average particle sizes of 2.50, 4.00, 3.90, 4.25 and 4.95 nm for B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs, respectively. Particle size shows wholly increasing trend from G-CDs to R-CDs, although G-, Y-CDs have similar sizes, indicating that the increase in particle size has some effect on the red shift of CDs, at least, on red shift of B-, G-, R-CDs [19]. Their HRTEM images exhibit well-resolved lattice fringes, ascribed to the (002) and (100) lattice planes of graphite, indicating that all CDs possess graphite-like structure [20].

XRD was measured to characterize the graphitic structure of CDs, as shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information). The XRD pattern of R-CDs shows a major diffraction peak at 23.5°, which is corresponding to carbon (002) plane, and two minor diffraction peaks at around 42.6° and 55.8°, which is assigned to carbon (100) and (004) planes, respectively. All the peaks indicate their graphitic nature [21]. Similarly, O-CDs show a carbon (002) peak at proximately 23.5°. A XRD profile of Y-CDs consists of a broad diffraction peak centered at around 23.5° and a sharp diffraction peak at 27.5°, indicating the formation of a graphene-like framework [22]. Compared with half-peak width of O-CDs, that of Y-CDs is broader, suggesting decreased graphitization of Y-CDs [23]. In XRD pattern of G-CDs, a weaker broad peak presents at a smaller diffraction angle of 19.8°, indicating further decreased graphitization of G-CDs [24]. This may be because that polymer chain and abundant functional groups enrich on the surface of G-CDs, which leads to the increase of the interval between layers [25].

Raman spectra was performed to further investigate the graphitization degree of CDs. The intensity ratio of G band at1638 cm−1 to D band at 1370 cm−1 (IG/ID) is 0.23, 1.05, 1.84, 2.30 and 2.39 for B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs, respectively (Fig. 2f). As it is known, the value of IG/ID stands for graphitization degree and ratio sp2/sp3 carbon. The increased values of IG/ID imply increased graphitized contents from B-CDs to R-CDs [26–28]. Although G-, Y-CDs do not exhibit significant differences in particle sizes, the graphitization degree of Y-CDs is higher than that of G-CDs. This means the size of conjugated sp2 domain and the corresponding graphitization degree increase from B-CDs to R-CDs. These variations are believed, at least partly, to be responsible for different emission wavelength of five CDs because higher graphitization degree or larger sp2 domain can result in decreased band gap, and further induce redshift of emission [29,30]. In addition, besides the D and G bands, the signal at 1286 cm−1 for G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs is assigned to carboxy groups on the surface of CDs [31].

To understand the role of functional groups of CDs in multicolor emission, EA, FT-IR, and XPS were employed. The EA results (Table S1, Supporting Information) show the N content decreases from G-CDs to R-CDs, which indicates red-shifted emission in this case is not caused by N-related defect states induced by N doping, as proved for other CDs [32,33]. From FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2g), the presence of N–H/O–H (3350 cm−1), CN (1688 cm−1), CC (1606 cm−1), C–N (1273 cm−1) and C–O (1147 cm−1) indicates the formation of aromatic structure of CDs [34,35]. A comparison of FT-IR spectra of samples reveals that B-CDs have different surface states with other four CDs, that is, G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs have carboxylic acid CO (1714 cm−1) but B-CDs do not, which is because B-CDs undergoes weak surface oxidation at low microwave power, resulting in the lack of COOH on the surface of B-CDs. The content of carboxylic acid CO gradually decreases from G-CDs to R-CDs. XPS was used to further explore surface compositions of CDs. It can be seen from full spectra of XPS (Fig. 2h) that five CDs show three peaks: C1s (285.1 eV), N1s (400.1 eV) and O1s (533.1 eV). From high-resolution XPS spectra and their relative element content (Fig. S2 and Table S2, Supporting Information), C1s XPS spectra of B-CDs are deconvoluted into two peaks including sp2 carbon (C–C/CC, 284.4 eV) and sp3 carbon (286.5 eV, C–O/C–N), while those of G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs are deconvoluted into three peaks assigned to sp2 carbon (C–C/CC, 284.3 eV), sp3 carbon (286.1 eV, C–O/C–N) and carbonyl carbon (O–CO, 288.9 eV), according with the FT-IR results [36]. N1s XPS spectra of five CDs are resolved into three peaks at 398.9, 399.7 and 401.5 eV, representing pyridinic, pyrrolic and graphic N, respectively [37]. The O1s XPS spectra of five CDs contain two peaks at 531.2 and 533.4 eV attributed to CO and C–O, respectively [38]. From G-CDs to R-CDs, the contents of sp2 carbon and carbonyl carbon regularly change (Table S3, Supporting Information). On one hand, C–C/CC content increases from 43.65% to 56.68%, revealing increased dehydrogenation degree from G-CDs to R-CDs. On the other hand, carbonyl carbon content gradually decreases from 19.66% to 0.35%, revealing decresed oxidation degree from G-CDs to R-CDs. Therefore, in this research, the emission of CDs is not caused by surface defect of CO [39–41]. On the basis of above analyses, it is inferred that the PL variation of five CDs is mainly associated with their graphitization degree or sp2 domain in this research. That is, higher graphitization degree or larger sp2 domain result in the redshift of emission from G-CDs to R-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical property and PL mechanism of B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Optical properties of five CDs were investigated. Unlike most other reported CDs, which exhibit nearly no fluorescence in solid state because of serious AIQ, five CDs in this work exhibit bright blue, green, yellow, orange and red solid-state fluorescence with excitation-independent property (Fig. 3 a and Figs. S3 and 4, Supporting Information). The emission peaks are centered at 445, 508, 546, 605 and 635 nm and maximum excitation peaks are 390, 445, 508, 560 and 595 nm for B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs, respectively (Fig. 3b and c). This is the first time, at least to our knowledge, to realize synthesis of full-color state-state fluorescent CDs without other additional dispersion processes (Table S4, Supporting Information). The bright solid-state fluorescence from CD powder is mainly related to their unique aggregated structure. When urea aqueous solution is heated, CO2 and NH3 volatile gas is generated from hydrolysis and decomposition, which promotes products to form a unique inflated foam structure, which isolates the nascent CDs, avoiding direct contact of CDs and thus inhibiting their AIQ effect [42]. Therefore, urea plays an important role for strong solid-state luminescence of CDs. In addition, the plenty carboxy groups hanging on the surface of CDs are responsible for solid-state emission by restraining π-π interaction [43]. Under optimum excitation wavelength, solid-state QY of CDs is 48.2%, 26.0%, 18.5%, 13.7% and 5.7% for B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs, respectively. In contrast, the QY of CDs prepared by PG without urea is only 3.5% (Fig. S5, Supporting Information), indicating the QY of CDs greatly increases by surface nitrogen doping [44]. The decreased QY from G-CDs to R-CDs is related to the decrease of nitrigen content and carboxy groups, as proved by Tables S2 and 3 (Supporting Information). Herein, B-CDs is exceptional case, which will be discussed later in detail. Average fluorescence lifetimes (τ) of CDs are determined to be 6.44, 4.76, 4.34, 2.77 and 2.33 ns for B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs, respectively, by time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) (Fig. 3d and Table S5, Supporting Information) [45]. The radiative decay rate/nonradiative decay rate (kr/knr) as well as average τ decrease as PL emission red-shifts, indicating increase of nonradiative traps, which is attributed to higher carbonization degree of longer-wavelength emissive CDs [46]. It can be seen that inceased carbonization, decreased nitrigen content and carboxy groups content together result in decreased solid-state QY from G-CDs to R-CDs.

In order to elucidate multicolor PL mechanism of CDs, optical property of five CDs in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) solutions with different concentrations was studied. From Fig. 4 a, G-, Y-, O-, R-CD dilute solution (0.05 mg/mL) exhibits blue emission with QY of 10.2%, 4.4%, 5.7% and 4.3%, respectively, and four CDs exhibit typical concentration-dependent emission. It can be seen that emission of CD solution gradually shifts to longer wavelength as the concentration of CD solution increases. Different from above four CDs, B-CD solution with diverse concentrations exhibits similar fluorescence (Fig. S6, Supporting Information). It can be seen that concentration-induced red shift is not applicable for B-CDs. From Fig. 4b–e, G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs exhibit different redshift although concentration of solution is same. To be specific, when the concentration of R-CD solution increases from 0.05 to 15 mg/mL until it becomes powder, the optimal emission peaks gradually shift from 442 to 603 until to 635 nm. The maximum emission of R-CD powder possesses remarkable red shift of 193 nm with respect to that of dilute solution (0.05 mg/mL). Similar with R-CDs, the emission peaks of O-, Y-, G-CD powders occur red shift of 166, 108 and 80 nm, respectively, with respect to those of their dilute solution. It is generally believed that concentration triggered redshift is caused by FRET or reabsorption [13].

Two factors determine whether FRET occurs in fluorescent system: one is severe spectral overlap between donor emission and acceptor absorption (generally, larger than 30%) [47], and the other is short distance between donor and acceptor (less than Förster radius, R0) [48]. Compared with UV–vis absorption spectra of G-, Y-, O-, R-CD dilute solution, those of concentrated solution and powder are significantly widened and extends to longer wavelengths (Fig. S7, Supporting Information), demonstrating the formation of G-, Y-, O-, R-CD aggregation in concentrated solution and powder [49]. This indicates the distance between nanoparticles would shorten and once the distance approaches R0, FRET probably takes place. While no obvious widened absorption was observed in B-CD concentrated solution. The overlaps between absorption of concentrated solution (15 mg/mL) and emission of dilute solution (0.05 mg/mL) were investigated. Those are 9.53%, 38.39%, 61.73%, 91.62% and 91.77% for B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs, respectively (Fig. 4f). Obviously, the overlap between absorption and emission spectra tends to increase from B-CDs to R-CDs, although O-, R-CDs show nearly similar extent of overlapping. The carbon nanomaterials with larger size of conjugated sp2 domain are more likely to act as strong FRET acceptors, contributing to the strong spectral overlap [50]. From B-CDs to R-CDs, the size of conjugated sp2 domain gradually increases, proved by TEM and Raman analyses, resulting in a gradual redshift of the absorption spectra and further greater overlap. The large overlap of G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs provides the possibility for FRET, but small overlap of B-CDs does not satisfy the condition of FRET occurrence (greater than 30% of overlap). Thus, it can be inferred that the difference in spectra overlaps is one reason for different FRET efficiency of five CDs, which is caused by increased size of conjugated sp2 domain from B-CDs to R-CDs. Further, FRET efficiency of CDs is sensitive to the distance between nano particles [51]. The zeta potential (ζ-potential) is −40.1, −29.0, −23.8 and −10.4 eV for G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs, respectively (Fig. 4g). The absolute value of ζ-potential gradually decreases from G-CDs to R-CDs, which results from the decreased content of carboxy groups according to FT-IR and XPS analyses, indicating weaker electrostatic repulsion. As a result, there is a shorter distance between nano particles in R-CDs than G-CDs when the concentration increases by the same amount. Therefore, the difference in distance between nano particles of CDs is the other reason for different FRET efficiency of CDs, which is induced by decreased content of carboxy groups from G-CDs to R-CDs. By taking into account of above-mentioned two factors, including spectral overlap and distance between nano particles, it is believed that FRET does not occur for B-CDs, but FRET occurs for G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs with gradually increased FRET efficiency from G-CDs to R-CDs.

Under varied excitation, the PL emission of G-, Y-, O-, R-CD solution at different concentrations and their powders were investigated to verify the FRET in G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs. As shown in Fig. S4 and S8–11 (Supporting Information), CD solution with low concentrations exhibits prominent excitation-dependent PL emission, revealing the presence of multiple luminescent centers in CDs. While concentrated CD solution and powder exhibit excitation-independent PL, indicating single emission center. When the concentration increases, the aggregation state forms, and the distance between multiple emitters is obviously decreased, which favors FRET among different luminescent centers [42]. Thus the short-wavelength emission is weakened and the long-wavelength emission is comparatively strengthened, resulting in significant red shift with increased concentration.

The FRET process in aggregated powder is also attested through TCSPC, and TCSPC of solid-state G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs were investigated at different emission wavelengths (Fig. S12 and Tables S6–S9, Supporting Information). Their average τ of is obviously dependent on emission wavelength. To be specific, τ varies from 2.59 to 5.15 ns for G-CDs, from 1.99 to 4.34 ns for Y-CDs, from 0.90 to 2.49 ns for O-CDs and from 0.49 to 2.49 ns for R-CDs as the probed emission wavelength increases. This is due to the fact that energy transfer process competes with radiative transition and nonradiative transition, leading to the increase in average τ and the red shift in emission wavelength [16,52]. Therefore, it is reasonable to suppose that FRET is the main origin of red-shift emission for G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs.

To further verify different FRET efficiencies of five CDs, TCSPC of dilute solution was further measured and compared with those of powders, as shown in Fig. 4h and Table S10 (Supporting Information). For B-CDs, the average τ of solution (3.57 ns) is shorter than that of powder (6.04 ns), indicating FRET does not occur, but the cases are opposite for G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs. The shorter average τ of powder than solution for G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs demonstrates the occurrence of FRET [53]. Specifically, as CDs change from solution to powder, τ varies from 2.91 to 2.62 ns for G-CDs, from 3.30 to 1.99 ns for Y-CDs, from 2.54 to 0.90 ns for O-CDs, and from 2.52 to 0.49 ns for R-CDs. It can be found that there is a more remarkable decrease of τ from G-CDs to R-CDs with change of CDs from solution to powder, indicating gradually increased FRET efficiency from G-CDs to R-CDs.

Different from the cases of other CDs, B-CD solutions maintain unchanged peaks at 412 and 437 nm at different concentrations (Fig. S13, Supporting Information). B-CD solution exhibits weak fluorescence with QY of 3.7%, while B-CD pristine powder shows bright blue fluorescence with solid-state QY of 48.2% and looks like flake crystalline grain (Fig. 5 a). After suffering from mechanical grounding and heating at 80 °C for 10 min, fluorescence intensity of powder significantly decreases, i.e., 20.0% of solid-state QY for ground powder and 5.3% of solid-state QY for heated powder. As shown in Fig. 5b, B-CD powders in three states (pristine, ground and heated) exhibit nearly the same emission peaks but gradually decreased PL intensity, consistent with gradually decreased QY. In order to understand difference of B-CD powders in three states, TCSPC was measured. As shown in Fig. 5c and Table S11 (Supporting Information), the pristine powder exhibits τ of 6.44 ns and corresponding high kr/knr (0.93), while ground powder and heated powder show τ of 6.11 and 5.84 ns and corresponding low kr/knr (0.25 and 0.06), implying that enhancement in solid-state QY for pristine powder is caused by blocking of nonradiative channels. As described in experiment, B-CD pristine crystal powder was obtained through quickly freezing B-CD solution, therefore there may be crystal water molecules in B-CDs pristine crystal powder, which was proved by TG and DTG analyses. The mass loss of the first stage from water is 15.4% in temperature range of 30–100 °C, and the first DTG peak observed around 80 °C is owing to weight loss process of crystal water (Fig. S14a, Supporting Information). The interactions between B-CDs and crystal water play a crucial role in keeping original solid-state crystallinity, and these interactions in pristine powder have locked and rigidified the molecular conformation, resulting in high solid-state QY [54]. Different from TG and DTG curves of pristine powder, there is no mass loss and no DTG peak in temperature range from 30 to 100 °C in ground and heated samples (Fig. S14b, Supporting Information). This indicates that crystal water was removed during grounding and heating, and the crystal structure was rearranged, leading to energy dissipation by nonradiative channels, and thus decrease of solid-state QY [55]. XRD was measured to demonstrate the change of crystal structure of B-CDs. The intense and sharp dirraction peaks were observed at the diffraction angle (2θ) of 13.06°, 22.48°, 27.22° and 39.76° in B-CD pristine powder, reflecting their crystalline order (Fig. S15, Supporting Information) [56,57]. In contrast, the realtive intensity of these diffraction peaks changes in B-CD ground powder, indicating the pristine crystal orientation changes, and moreover, the diffraction peaks of B-CD heated powder are wider, indicating heated sample was present in semi-amorphous phase [54,58]. These observations suggest that original crystalline structures of B-CDs have been changed by mechanical force and thermal interaction. Therefore, B-CDs show unique crystal-induced emission enhancement. This conclusion is consistent with recent reports, where researchers proposed that high crystalline can trigger strong photoluminescence [59,60].

On the basis of above analyses, the solid-state multicolor luminescence mechanism of five CDs is explained, as shown in Fig. 6 . At low microwave power (i.e.,144 W), the products remain solution state because of low carbonization degree, which is followed by crystal formation after shock cooling. As-obtained crystal sample emits bright blue solid-state fluorescence because of small size of conjugated sp2 domain. B-CDs exhibit high solid-state QY owing to unique crystal-induced emission enhancement. At 288 W or larger, high microwave power and low urea content favor sample carbonization and increased graphilization, therefore, the products change from solution to solid. During the formation of powder product, the space structures of G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs are compressed and thus the distance between nano particles is decreased. Both of small distance and severe spectral overlap induce the occurrence of FRET. Further, the increased conjugated sp2 domain results in larger spectral overlap from G-CDs to R-CDs, and the decreased content of carbonxy groups results in shorter distance between nano particles from G-CDs to R-CDs. Both of them synergistically lead to gradually enhanced FRET efficiency, resulting in red-shifted emission from G-CDs to R-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs in multicolor/white LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: Given the excellent solid-state luminescence of five CDs, CDs are used as phosphors combing with UV chip to prepare multiple LEDs, including multicolor and white LEDs. Seen from Fig. 7 a–c, B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-LEDs exhibit luminous blue, green, yellow, orange and red light with CIE coordinates of (0.20, 0.18), (0.31, 0.44), (0.48, 0.50), (0.52, 0.45) and (0.68, 0.31), respectively.

Then, white LEDs with adjustable CCT and high CRI were fabricated using B-, G-, R-CDs as phosphors and UV chip as base by varying ratio of B-, G-, R-CDs and working voltages of devices. When the mass ratio of B-, G-, R-CDs was 273.6:1.0:0.7 and working voltage was set at 3.5 V, the device showed cold white light with CCT of 8588 K, CIE coordinates of (0.29, 0.31) and CRI of 88, which provides the possibility for outdoor lighting (Fig. 6d1–f1). When mass ratio of B-, G-, R-CDs was kept 273.6:1.0:0.7 and working voltage was changed at 3.3 V, pure white LED was obtained with CCT of 6104 K, CIE coordinates of (0.32, 0.36) and CRI of 82 (Fig. 6d2–f2). When mass ratio of B-, G-, R-CDs was changed to 23.8:1.0:0.7 and working voltage of LED was 3.5 V, white LED exhibited warm light with CCT of 3937 K, CIE coordinates of (0.39, 0.40) and CRI of 80 (Fig. 6d3–f3), which meets the requirement of indoor illuminates [31]. In the practical application of LEDs, the used-phosphors are often exposed to UV light for a long time, thus the good light stability of phosphors is necessary. The photostability of B-, G-, R-CDs was investigated. After 60 min of irradiation, the PL intensity of B-CDs keeps nearly constant, and those of G-, R-CDs keeps 85.5% and 75.0% of initial intensity, which demonstrates the good light stability of B-, G-, R-CDs (Fig. S16, Supporting information). The emission spectra of warm white LED are fitted to three peaks located at 455, 555 and 600 nm, belonging to blue, green and red light regions (Fig. S17a, Supporting information). Compared with the emission of single B-, G-, R-CDs (445, 508, and 635 nm), the fitting peaks of blue and green light regions exhibit red shift ascribed to re-absorption, while that of red light region shows blue shift because of low concentration loading [9]. In order to explore if a strong energy transfer occurred B-, G-, R-CDs, the average τ of RGB phosphors was also detected at 455, 555 and 600 nm (Fig. S17b−d and Table S12, Supporting information). For RGB phosphors, the average τ at 455 and 555 nm is 5.97 and 4.17 ns, which is slightly lower than that of single B-, G-CDs (6.44 and 4.76 ns), and the average τ at 600 nm is 3.72 ns, which is higher than that of single R-CDs (2.33 ns). The less obvious change in τ indicates weak energy transfer occurred between B-, G- R-CDs, demonstrating relatively good stability of white LEDs [61]. In this study, CCT-adjustable white LEDs from 3937 to 8588 K have been realized, which provides a practical foundation for the application of multicolor solid-state luminescent CDs in multiple LEDs.

In conclusion, we developed a new system for the synthesis of CDs with tunable full-color solid-state emission via pyrolysis of PG and urea. The emission of CDs can be tuned from blue to red by adjusting a microwave power and ratio of precursors. The increase in both power and ratio of PG/urea synergistically leads to large conjugated sp2 size and high graphitization degree of CDs. The multicolor fluorescence mechanism of CDs was proposed. G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs exhibit aggregation-induced red shift emission. The large spectra overlap and aggregated induced short interparticle distances trigger FRET for G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs. Further, increased size of conjugated sp2 domain and decreased carbonxy content synergistically induce enhanced FRET efficiency, resulting in red-shifted emission from G-CDs to R-CDs. Different with these four CDs, B-CDs show unique crystal-induced emission enhancement. B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-CDs exhibit PL emission peaked at 445, 508, 545, 595 and 635 nm with solid-state QY of 48.2%, 26.0%, 18.5%, 13.7% and 5.7%, respectively. Finally, the application of solid-state CDs in LEDs was demonstrated by taking advantages of outstanding optical properties of CDs. Multicolor LEDs and multiple types of white LEDs with adjustable CCT from 3937 to 8588 K and high CRI of 80–88 were constructed by varying ratio of B-, G-, R-CDs and working voltage.

Junli Wang: Preparation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jingxia Zheng: Methodology. Yongzhen Yang: Writing - review & editing. Xuguang Liu: Writing - review & editing. Jieshan Qiu: Supervision. Yue Tian: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1710117 and 51972221) and Research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2020–051 and HGKY2019027).
